speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Schillinger, you were just describing the moment when the doors on the box cars were opened and the reactions of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the soldiers who were in active fighting unit were taking a chocolate Hershey bars from their pockets and throwing it to us, and cigarettes, and, and whatever they had-- chewing gum and canned food, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as good as they could be under the circumstances, as wonderful as they, they could be. And they really were to us the saviors and the angels. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Soon, a, a major whose name was Dr. Julius Rock-- R-O-C-K-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who was a medical officer in the unit-- and this [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unit belonged to the 30th Infantry unit of the 9th Army. This Dr. Rock ordered to evacuate a town. The name was Hillersleben. And this whole area is near [INAUDIBLE], Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He evacuated this town. Under threat, the mayor complied. And they put up us-- us up in that town, one or two, two families in a, in a home. And you can imagine what it meant to a home, to sleep in a bed, to, to eat some food that, that it was human, that was edible. The American army also set up a field hospital, I think for 1,000 beds, that I was immediately hospitalized and diagnosed as bilateral tuberculosis. I had TB in both of my lungs with pleurisy. That's why the stabbing pains. And [SIGHS] in, in itself, this is a death sentence in 1945, because there was no cure for tuberculosis. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was treated by German doctors also, who were forced by the, by the Americans to treat us and take care of us. The American soldiers would come and visit us. And we made acquaintance with them. And my sister fell in love with, with an American soldier, just as you see in the movies. It was a wonderful experience. One day, we heard more rumbling and more cannon fires. And the fear grabbed me again. And we were terribly afraid of what's, what's going to happen. Maybe the Germans are coming back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And an American GI was posted next to our house. He were standing there. I still remember him with chewing gum and standing there with his gun. And I said to him-- because I knew a little English from my teacher, Schporer, the fencer-- Germans come back? And he looked at me, said, never, never.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were the English words I remember that they were told to me. And I also remember an American doctor with his gold-rimmed glasses looking over me and listening to my chest in that field hospital. And his name was Dr. White. And he pinched me. He says, no flesh, because there was no flesh. I was 30 kilograms. And they treated us. They gave us clothing. The Americans put us together and then put big hoses in our clothing and sprayed us with DDT. Everybody smelled DDT. And finally, we got rid of, of our lice. A small cemetery was started next to this Hillersleben where we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sheltered. And this little cemetery was growing by the day. I would say half of our people who were there liberated died afterwards of typhus, of tuberculosis, or pneumonia, or I don't know what, or just weakness, or elementary problems. A few weeks later, I became yellow completely. So I acquired hepatitis in addition to my tuberculosis. And I'm still alive. There was no treatment for that, either. It's-- it was hepatitis. A, I think. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] slowly, slowly, the good food that the Americans provided us, the hope or the-- my, my parents help, I, I can become better and better. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's hard to believe, but by the time 1946 came in Hungary, my tuberculosis spontaneously cured itself. All my lesions were calcified. And I lived. That's one of the few or many miracles that happened during those times. Those homes that we stayed, actually, just to, to clear my conscience some people had to leave their homes for me, even they were Germans, were for high-ranking German SS officers and their families, which were built that whole community by the Nazi government to, to put this people. We found the photographs of these people in their uniforms and, and Nazi reliquary everywhere, and Nazi flags, and medals, and whatnot. So my conscience is clear. I think we deserved to be in those places.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there during the summer, the whole summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1945-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] constant contact with the American troops. And one day, the Americans told us that they would had to leave the place-- this was the Elbe River area-- because according to the agreement, the Soviet troops will take over. And the Americans will go back west. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We wanted to go home. They wanted to go back to Hungary to, to see what happens, if our family is still there, or-- and we were curious. The Americans said, you could come with us if you wanted. But we chose to stay. So they lowered the American flag. And the Russian troops came in. They raised the red flag. And there was another strange uniform, which I have never seen before. They were rather filthy compared to the neat, clean American boys. But it doesn't matter. They were rather rude. They came and broke into our homes, some of them drunk. They demanded [RUSSIAN SPEECH], which is watch in Russian. They were collecting watches. And in a short while, we realized it was a pretty big mistake to stay. But we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, they gathered us. And they put us in a camp, another camp. It was a concentration camp, not with barbed wires and, and towers. It was a, a made-- a makeshift camp. And there were also [? took ?] strange peoples-- Germans and Hungarians and Hungarian non-Jews. But very soon, we learned they were ex-Nazis who did not go back to Hungary but stayed in the west.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it just-- it looked awful, the situation. And it appeared to us that this is some, someplace that somehow you have to get away. And one day, somebody told us-- maybe it was a rumor, or maybe it's the truth-- that taking everybody to Siberia. That we were Jews, that we came from Bergen-Belsen didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father decided that we'll do our best to get away from here. So that night when we learned about this news, he gathered our little family. And at night, we climbed over the fence in an, an area which was not guarded and ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: We ran in to the railroad station. There were coal cars. And we jumped on the coal cars. And we took off from there. And on those coal cars--

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you jumped on the coal cars? What, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed on top of the coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: On top of the coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this train [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reversed my curve of the train ride leading me to horrible things. It was beginning to lead us to better things at that time. The train went to Czechoslovakia, to Brno, to [? Brno, ?] where we change another train and arrived to Prague. And from spending a couple of days in Prague, eventually would return to Hungary. We were stopping people on the street in Prague. And since we didn't-- I didn't speak any and my father didn't, we tried in German. And they told us, don't speak German, no German, no Deutsche. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went home. And we arrived to Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remembered when we left Hungary and we were asking for water from the wagon. And here I am, back again. In that country, hopefully things will be better. So on the railroad station at the border, I saw a large cauldron on fire. And a big, fat peasant woman was stirring that soup, which smelled deliciously. And I, I was terribly hungry and terribly skinny. And I went to that lady. And I said to her, lady, I just came back from a concentration camp. I'm 15 years old. Could you spare me a, a cup of soup? I'm terribly hungry. And she kept stirring that cauldron. I still see her. And then she says, this is only for our boys. They were waiting for prisoners of war. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I left. And I realized that nothing really changed. It is, it is so deeply encrusted, there is nothing will change this. And later on when I became a physician, I, I felt that how could I treat some of these people? I have to go away somewhere. Let me ask you, what did you-- how long did you stay in, in Hungary before leaving? How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the fall of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we returned there. I went to-- back to high school, to the same wonderful high school with great teachers. I took two years in one year. I tried to recoup what I lost. And eventually, I graduated from that high school. I went to medical school in, in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year did you graduate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you graduate?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to that [SIGHS] medical school. There was 1,500 applicants. And they took us, 300 of us. And, and I thought felt if not for this principal in the high school, I would, would have never gotten into medical school. And when I decided to become a, a doctor was in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought to myself that if I ever live, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I will become a doctor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed in Hungary until 1956. Practicing medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Practicing two years-- one and a half years of medicine after graduation in my small town, the same town where, where we lived before. And then the Hungarian Revolution came. And the borders were opened and-- sort of-- and then I escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. In other words, would you have left earlier if you had been able to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I always wanted to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always hated the communists. I always hated the Russians because of my early experiences. I, I could never buy what they told us. Because I knew. I knew that it's not true what they talked about the Americans. It's not new that the imperialists were the dogs and, and everybody will be oppressed and suppressed. And I knew they were good people from my personal experience. I had an American flag in my attic hidden, in little one, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] which I cherished. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So I left after the revolution. It was eventually the spring of 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out? Did you-- let me ask first, did your-- the rest of your family get out with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my parents remained in Budapest. And my sister and I tried to escape. And we were captured at the border. And they put us in jail in, in Czechoslovakia for a couple of days, in solitary jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they sent us back to Hungary. And in Hungary, again, put in a prison of Russians, or the Russian army's prison. And I, I was questioned and interviewed by a Russian major with the golden teeth. And he said, with an interpreter, said, you're a doctor? Said, yes. Why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it was too much war here. I wanted to go to a peaceful country, to Austria. Huh, said, he says to me, I spit on you Hungarian intellectuals. And he spat in my face. And I took the list when my name was on it and put the red circle. He said, you once more you try, I shoot you. OK. And then they let me go. Next morning, I tried with my sister-- next morning, 50 kilometers north of where that major [LAUGHS] happened to be. And at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we gave a--

speaker: interviewer
text: On foot, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: On, foot, yeah-- we gave a, a gold chain to, to somebody who guided us to the border. And, and two hills before the border, he said, go two hills. And I'm not going there. But you will see a path, which was plowed. And that's the border. You will see towers there. I don't know whether there are soldiers there. I don't know whether there are mines there. But I tell, you this is the border. You go through, you will be in Austria. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister and me, a Catholic priest, and another family, we went with single files. We ran across. Nothing exploded. Nobody shot, and all ran across. And there was some people there. And I asked them, ist das Oesterreich? He said, ja, ja. And we were free. How did you make your way to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very circuitous way-- my mother passed away when we were in, in Vienna with my sister. And we were waiting to come to the USA. And in the Israeli embassy, they told us that if you come to Israel, if you go to Israel, we will bring your father out. So we immediately signed up. And, and we went to Israel. And sure enough, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think in four or five months later, they brought my father to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a physician, fortunately. Since I left in a shirt, all I had is what's, what's in my brain. And I told Israelis I was a doctor. And I don't have any papers, no diploma. And they said, we'll put you in a hospital. You work there. In the few months, we'll know whether you're a doctor or not. And they put me in a, a hospital [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where there was chest surgery and tuberculotic patients, which I was not afraid of, since I, I was immune. And I didn't speak any Hebrew. They told me that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can speak English if you speak. And for six months, I did. And then they told me, from now on, it's all Hebrew. You write Hebrew. You speak Hebrew. You treat in Hebrew. And when my father arrived, he brought my diploma out from Hungary. So I was quite legitimate at that time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an ulpan, which is a, a night study. And fortunately, I met my wife, who spoke Hebrew. And she taught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yoel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her, her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zahava, Zahava Yoel. And she was a, a Chilean born from San-- Santiago de Chile. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very trivial-- in the swimming pool.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the swim--

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in the swimming pool, and when she had a day off and, and I had also day off. And then an older gentleman who was with me, he introduced us. And that what happened. So that, that somehow, my, my whole life has been paved with fortunate accidents, sometimes not so the misfortunate, that the fact that my mother died, that's why I went to Israel. And if I had not gone to Israel, I would have never met her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was a great experience, to be in Israel for five years. And it really had nothing to do with life or economics that I came here. I always wanted to come to the United States after that experience of liberation. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And my mother also always pumped it in me, you should go. When you grow up, you should go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What was, what was her feeling and what was your feeling about the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. For the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from your experience with the liberators, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the-- well, she had personal connections. Her, her father lived here in Brooklyn, my grandpa, and her sisters and brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we always had the American connection, so to speak,

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was there anything else, any kind of-- anything ideological or anything that you knew about American life or thought about American life that would have brought you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really didn't know much. All I, all I knew, this was a, a west and free life compared to communism, obviously. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I saw nothing negative about this country. I still don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to the United States. Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in Westchester County to White Plains, White Plains Hospital. I was an intern there. And then, having had five years of surgical experience in Israel, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I took a position in, in Buffalo, New York, in a medical school, and take a residency in urology. I wanted to get a new specialty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I became a urologist in, in Buffalo, New York, where we lived until my retirement in 1972. And in 1972, we moved to, to Florida, to Sarasota, where I have a place. And in the summers, we spent still in Westchester County, which was my first love in the USA. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1995, on the 50th anniversary of, of my liberation, I wrote a letter to the editor in the paper in Sarasota, which is owned by And in, in a brief letter, I, I described my liberation and who the people were who liberated me and to give a public thanks to the US Army. You can't believe the response. I, I got letters, telephone calls. And, and I still keep contact. All the people, I took them out for dinner. We were all together. And there was a colonel who was in the 30th Infantry Unit there who is 75 years old now who became my friend. And he's my bicycle partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, so now you divide your time between Florida and, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel about living in the United States at this point? You've already expressed some very positive feelings, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a cliche to say this is the greatest country in the world. It's a cliche it's the greatest form of government in the world, but it is true. I, I had the disprivilege of experiencing fascism. I experienced Nazism in Hungary, the German system in Germany, the communism afterwards. I lived through quite a few regimes and different systems. And this is the only one that you can live. This is the only one, this is acceptable to, to normal human life. It's a wonderful country.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children? And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children. Don, my oldest, is in, in U-Penn in Philadelphia. He works there. And Dean, he's a physician in San Francisco, connected with the university-- works at San Francisco General. And his wife is Ariella, who is a lawyer in San Francisco. And Ben, who lives in Philadelphia, and he teaches English at Berlitz. And he's not married yet, Don married in Israel to Karen. And all my children and their wives are wonderful.
